description: Persistently elevated blood pressure that can damage the heart and blood vessels over time.
A range of conditions affecting the heart's structure and function, including coronary artery disease.
Sudden blockage of blood flow to part of the heart muscle, causing tissue damage.
Abnormal heart rhythm caused by irregular electrical signals in the heart.
Condition in which the heart cannot pump blood efficiently enough to meet the body's needs.
Buildup of fatty plaques inside artery walls, narrowing blood flow over time.
A blood clot that forms in a deep vein, usually in the leg, which can be dangerous if it travels to the lungs.
Narrowing of arteries that reduces blood flow to the limbs, most often the legs.
Disease of the heart muscle that makes it harder for the heart to pump blood effectively.
Recurrent, often severe headaches frequently accompanied by nausea and sensory sensitivity.
Neurological disorder causing recurrent, unprovoked seizures.
Interruption of blood supply to part of the brain, causing rapid loss of brain function.
Progressive nervous system disorder affecting movement, often starting with a tremor in one hand.
Autoimmune disease in which the immune system attacks the protective covering of nerve fibers.
Sudden weakness or paralysis of muscles on one side of the face due to nerve dysfunction.
Damage to peripheral nerves causing weakness, numbness, and pain, usually in the hands and feet.
The most common type of headache, causing mild to moderate dull, aching pain across the head.
A sensation of spinning or movement, often caused by inner ear or neurological issues.
Chronic pain condition causing sudden, severe facial pain along the trigeminal nerve.
Chronic inflammatory condition of the airways causing recurring breathing difficulty.
Infection that inflames the air sacs in one or both lungs, often filling them with fluid.
Progressive lung disease that causes airflow blockage and breathing-related problems.
Inflammation of the bronchial tubes that carry air to the lungs, causing cough and mucus.
Bacterial infection primarily affecting the lungs, spread through airborne droplets.
A blockage in one of the pulmonary arteries in the lungs, usually caused by a blood clot.
Inflammation of the lining around the lungs, causing sharp pain with breathing.
Sleep disorder in which breathing repeatedly stops and starts during sleep.
Inflammation of the stomach lining, often causing pain and digestive discomfort.
Inflammation of the stomach and intestines typically caused by viral or bacterial infection.
Chronic condition where stomach acid flows back into the esophagus, causing irritation.
Open sores that develop on the inner lining of the stomach or upper small intestine.
Common disorder affecting the large intestine causing cramping, bloating, and altered bowel habits.
Chronic inflammation of the digestive tract, including Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis.
Hardened deposits of digestive fluid that form in the gallbladder, sometimes blocking bile flow.
Inflammation of the pancreas that can cause severe abdominal pain and digestive problems.
Autoimmune disorder in which eating gluten triggers an immune response damaging the small intestine.
Swollen veins in the lower rectum and anus causing discomfort and occasional bleeding.
Inflammation of the appendix that typically causes pain starting near the navel and shifting to the lower right abdomen.
Chronic skin condition causing inflamed, itchy, and cracked patches of skin.
Autoimmune condition causing rapid skin cell buildup, resulting in scaly, itchy patches.
Common skin condition occurring when hair follicles become clogged with oil and dead skin cells.
Also known as hives; raised, itchy welts on the skin often triggered by an allergic reaction.
Bacterial infection of the deeper layers of skin, causing redness, swelling, and warmth.
Viral infection causing a painful rash, caused by reactivation of the chickenpox virus.
Infection of the skin caused by fungi, often presenting as itchy, scaly, ring-shaped patches.
Condition causing loss of skin pigment, resulting in white patches on various parts of the body.
Chronic condition affecting blood sugar regulation.
Underactive thyroid gland producing insufficient thyroid hormone, slowing metabolism.
Overactive thyroid gland producing excess thyroid hormone, speeding up metabolism.
Condition caused by prolonged exposure to high cortisol levels in the body.
Disorder in which the adrenal glands do not produce enough hormones.
Hormonal disorder common among women of reproductive age, affecting ovulation and metabolism.
Excess body fat accumulation that increases the risk of numerous chronic diseases.
Inflammation of one or more joints causing pain and stiffness.
Condition in which bones become weak and brittle, increasing fracture risk.
Degenerative joint disease caused by wear and tear of cartilage over time.
Autoimmune disorder in which the immune system attacks the joints, causing inflammation.
Form of arthritis caused by excess uric acid forming sharp crystals in a joint, often the big toe.
Pain that radiates along the path of the sciatic nerve, from the lower back down the leg.
Condition where the soft cushion between spinal bones pushes out, often pressing on nearby nerves.
Inflammation or irritation of a tendon, usually caused by repetitive movement or overuse.
A break in a bone, ranging from a thin crack to a complete break, usually due to trauma.
Mood disorder causing persistent feelings of sadness and loss of interest in activities.
Mental health condition characterized by persistent, excessive worry and nervousness.
Mental health condition marked by extreme mood swings between emotional highs and lows.
Disorder characterized by recurring, unexpected panic attacks and fear of future attacks.
Disorder featuring unwanted recurring thoughts and repetitive behaviors performed to reduce anxiety.
Mental health condition triggered by experiencing or witnessing a traumatic event.
Persistent difficulty falling or staying asleep despite adequate opportunity to sleep.
Hard mineral deposits that form in the kidneys and can cause significant pain when passing.
Gradual loss of kidney function over time, often caused by diabetes or high blood pressure.
Bacterial infection affecting any part of the urinary system, most often the bladder.
Inflammation of the kidney's filtering units, which can impair kidney function.
Bacterial infection that has spread to one or both kidneys, often from the bladder.
Genetic disorder causing numerous fluid-filled cysts to develop in the kidneys.
Liver infection caused by the hepatitis A virus, usually spread through contaminated food or water.
Serious liver infection caused by the hepatitis B virus, which can become chronic.
Liver infection caused by the hepatitis C virus, often leading to chronic liver disease.
Accumulation of excess fat in the liver, which can lead to inflammation and liver damage.
Late-stage liver scarring caused by long-term liver damage, impairing liver function.
Yellowing of the skin and eyes caused by elevated bilirubin levels, usually due to liver dysfunction.
Contagious viral respiratory infection causing fever, body aches, and fatigue.
Mild viral infection of the upper respiratory tract.
Infectious respiratory illness caused by the SARS-CoV-2 virus, with widely varying severity.
Mosquito-borne viral infection causing high fever and severe body aches.
Mosquito-borne parasitic infection causing recurring fever and flu-like illness.
Bacterial infection spread through contaminated food and water, causing sustained high fever.
Highly contagious viral infection causing an itchy, blister-like rash and fever.
Highly contagious viral infection causing fever, cough, and a characteristic red rash.
Viral infection primarily affecting the salivary glands, causing facial swelling near the jaw.
Bacterial infection transmitted through the bite of an infected tick.
Life-threatening, extreme immune response to infection that can damage organs.
Deficiency of healthy red blood cells leading to reduced oxygen delivery to body tissues.
The most common form of anemia, caused by insufficient iron to produce hemoglobin.
Cancer of the blood-forming tissues, including bone marrow and the lymphatic system.
Genetic disorder impairing the body's ability to form blood clots, leading to excessive bleeding.
Condition characterized by abnormally low levels of platelets in the blood.
Inflammation of the sinuses, often following a cold or due to allergies, causing facial pressure.
Inflammation of the tonsils, usually caused by viral or bacterial infection.
Middle ear infection, common in children, often following a respiratory infection.
Inflammation of the throat (pharynx), commonly known as a sore throat.
Allergic inflammation of the nasal airways triggered by allergens such as pollen or dust.
Inner ear disorder causing episodes of vertigo, hearing loss, and ringing in the ears.
Inflammation of the voice box (larynx) often causing hoarseness or temporary loss of voice.
Bleeding from the nose, commonly caused by dry air, irritation, or minor trauma.
Inflammation of the membrane covering the eye, commonly known as pink eye.
Clouding of the eye's natural lens, leading to gradual, painless vision loss.
Group of eye conditions that damage the optic nerve, often related to high eye pressure.
Age-related condition that damages the macula, causing loss of central vision.
Condition in which the eyes don't produce enough tears or the tears evaporate too quickly.
Serious condition in which the retina pulls away from its normal position at the back of the eye.
A small, painful lump on the eyelid caused by an infected oil gland.
Severe, life-threatening allergic reaction that can develop within minutes of exposure to an allergen.
Illness caused by eating food contaminated with bacteria, viruses, or toxins.
Complex disorder characterized by extreme fatigue that doesn't improve with rest.
Chronic disorder causing widespread musculoskeletal pain along with fatigue and tenderness.
Headache caused by pressure from sinus inflammation, often confused with migraine.
Viral infection commonly spread through saliva, causing prolonged fatigue and sore throat.
Bacterial infection of the throat and tonsils caused by group A Streptococcus.
Compression of the median nerve in the wrist, causing numbness and tingling in the hand.
Enlarged, twisted veins usually appearing in the legs due to weakened vein valves.
Inflammation of the cartilage connecting the ribs to the breastbone, causing chest pain.
Inflammation or infection of small pouches that can form in the lining of the intestine.
Common viral infection of the small airways in the lungs, most common in infants.
Highly contagious bacterial respiratory infection causing severe, prolonged coughing fits.
Contagious skin infestation caused by tiny mites that burrow into the skin.
Chronic skin condition causing facial redness and visible blood vessels, often with bumps.
A collapse or crush injury of a spinal bone, commonly caused by osteoporosis.
Infection or inflammation of the outer ear canal, often called swimmer's ear.
Sudden episode of kidney failure or damage occurring within hours or days.
Chronic disease in which the immune system attacks the liver, causing inflammation.
Common viral illness in young children causing sores in the mouth and a rash on hands and feet.
